The adoption of Enterprise AI has prevented active deployment in 42% of large companies (over 1,000 employees), while 40% are stuck in exploration or experimentation phases and cannot bring models into production.
The gap between early adopters accelerating investment and organisations unable to cross the entry thresholds is a growing competitive gap requiring immediate attention from business leaders.

Over the past 24 months, 59% of companies already using or exploring AI have accelerated their investments, with China (85%), India (74%) and the United Arab Emirates (72%) recording the highest rates of acceleration, while Canada (35%), Australia (38%) and the United Kingdom (40%) have slowed down.
Geographical differences in active employment are strong: India (59%) and the UAE (58%) are well above France (26%), Australia (29%) and Spain (28%).
Financial services lead to industrial adoption at around 50% of active introductions compared to 37% in telecommunications.
The primary obstacles blocking the next wave are a skill gap (33% quote limited AI expertise), data complexity (25%) and ethical concerns (23%).
One in five organisations lacks employees with the right skills and 16% cannot find qualified new jobs.

Although 85% of IT professionals believe that consumers prefer companies with transparent AI practices, less than half have taken concrete steps: only 27% actively reduce bias, 37% pursue data excellence and 44% have developed ethical AI policies.
Generative AI faces even steeper trust barriers, with 57% of non-adopters referring to data protection and 43% to transparency concerns.

Cost reduction and process automation (42%) and more accessible tools (45%) are the leading adoption drivers, while R & D (44%) and personnel skills (39%) are the leading investment sectors – to date, only 34% of organizations are training employees to work alongside AI.

Organisations should prioritize the closure of the skills gap through structured reskilling programs and simplified deployment tools, as the workforce's willingness is the largest barrier and only a third of companies are actively addressing it despite the acceleration in accessible AI tools, which make it increasingly possible for prepared teams to be deployed.
